name: Backpropagation
tagline: Efficient gradient computation for layered models via the chain rule
problem:
Neural networks and layered models have many parameters. Computing gradients naively is prohibitively expensive if you treat each parameter independently.

Backpropagation solves this by reusing intermediate computations: apply the chain rule backward through the computation graph to compute gradients efficiently.

The remaining problem for Backpropagation is operational reliability. A reader needs more than a name and a historical note: they need to know what failure this capability prevents, what resources it consumes, what signs show that it is failing, and when it is safer to stop than to improvise. This entry therefore treats backpropagation as a teachable system with inputs, inspection points, records, and limits rather than as a single trick.
overview:
Backprop treats a model as a sequence of operations. A forward pass computes outputs and caches intermediates; a backward pass propagates error signals and computes gradients for each parameter.

The method is general: any differentiable computation graph can use it. Practical engineering issues include numerical stability, gradient explosion/vanishing, and correct implementation of derivatives.

In collapse recovery, backpropagation must be repairable with local materials, teachable to more than one person, and documented well enough that the method survives injury, migration, or leadership change. In a space-settlement context, the same capability must account for closed-loop resources, contamination control, spare-part scarcity, power budgets, and the cost of mistakes in a sealed habitat. The practical minimum is a controlled trial, written checks, safe storage, and review before scale-up.

principles.name: Chain rule
principles.explanation: Derivatives of composed functions multiply; backprop organizes this computation efficiently.

principles.name: Computation graphs
principles.explanation: Represent complex functions as nodes/edges so gradients can be computed systematically.

principles.name: Automatic differentiation
principles.explanation: Software implements backprop for arbitrary graphs; correctness requires careful testing.

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what Backpropagation is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

components.name: Input control point
components.function: Checks materials, tools, information, and environmental conditions before work starts
components.position: At receiving, storage, or setup area
components.madeFrom: Labels, inspection notes, gauges, samples, and quarantine bins
components.criticalNote: Unknown inputs should be isolated until checked.

rawMaterials.name: Electric power
rawMaterials.purpose: Energy for computation, storage, and networking
earthLocations: Electrical grids globally; generators in remote sites
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Solar + batteries is the simplest early-space pathway; plan for tight energy budgets and thermal rejection.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Stable voltage regulation, grounding, and basic power-quality control.

rawMaterials.name: Semiconductor devices
rawMaterials.purpose: Transistors and ICs that perform switching, storage, and signal conditioning
earthLocations: Global semiconductor supply chains; packaged IC distributors
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Early colonies import packaged devices; long-term local manufacture requires clean chemistry and crystal growth capabilities.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: ESD-safe handling; correct packaging, heat sinking, and derating for temperature and radiation.

rawMaterials.name: Documentation and standards
rawMaterials.purpose: Make designs reproducible and debuggable across teams and time
earthLocations: Libraries, universities, standards bodies, archived web
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Mirror core references offline; maintain printed and digital copies with checksums and redundancy.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Versioning, change control, and clear measurement units and assumptions.

rawMaterials.name: Test cases
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Same requirement: treat tests as life-support for software reliability.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Finite-difference gradient checks and regression tests.

buildSteps.title: Write forward computation clearly
buildSteps.description: Define layers/operations; cache intermediates needed for derivatives.

buildSteps.title: Implement backward derivatives
buildSteps.description: Compute local derivatives; propagate gradients backward through the graph.
buildSteps.warningNote: Small sign/shape bugs silently break training.

buildSteps.title: Validate with gradient checks
buildSteps.description: Compare analytic gradients to finite differences on small models.
buildSteps.warningNote: Only checking loss curves is not proof of correctness.

buildSteps.title: Stabilize training
buildSteps.description: Add normalization, clipping, or better initialization when gradients explode/vanish.

buildSteps.title: Define the local purpose and stop conditions
buildSteps.description: Write down why Backpropagation is needed, who is responsible, what success looks like, and which conditions require stopping or escalation.
buildSteps.warningNote: Do not start a life-critical or infrastructure-critical use without clear stop conditions.

history.event: Backprop and related methods formalize and spread in ML.
Automatic differentiation frameworks make backprop ubiquitous.
Scaling requires systems engineering and careful numerics.

impact:
Backpropagation enables deep learning by making gradient computation tractable for large models. It turns learning into an optimization problem on a computation graph.

In The Codex, backprop is the hinge between math and modern AI capability; it depends on disciplined software and testing culture.

images.caption: Forward pass, loss, chain rule, gradients, update

sources.title: Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams (1986) — Learning representations by back-propagating errors
Goodfellow et al. — Deep Learning (backprop chapter)
